claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 20 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 20, which is 20 or over
circumstances: Child is 20, which is 20 or over
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin, I need to understand your situation. 

**First, let's talk about the child or children you're asking about:**

1. **How old is your child?** (Or if you have multiple children, please tell me about each of their ages)

This is important because:
- Children under 16 generally qualify automatically (with some conditions)
- Children aged 16-19 may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children aged 20 or over typically don't qualify

Please share the age(s) of the child/children you're asking about.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex is 8 years old, and Blake is 20.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'm happy to help you with your Child Benefit eligibility for both Alex and Blake.

Let me work through each child:

**For Alex (8 years old):**
Since Alex is under 16, let me ask a few questions to check eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?** 

(For an 8-year-old, this would typically be "yes" - they're not in paid work)

And while we're discussing Alex, I also need to know:

**Is Alex receiving any out-of-work benefits in their own name** (such as Income Support, Universal Credit, or Employment and Support Allowance)?

---

**For Blake (20 years old):**
Unfortunately, children aged 20 or over generally do not qualify for Child Benefit, as the upper age limit is 19 (or just after their 20th birthday in certain education circumstances).

Let me confirm the details for Alex first, and then I'll provide you with a complete assessment for both children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex - no, she doesn't work at all, she's only 8. And no, she doesn't receive any benefits in her own name.

As for Blake, I understand - I thought it might be worth checking anyway.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that information about Alex!

Let me continue with Alex's assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex in any type of apprenticeship program?**

(Again, for an 8-year-old, this would typically be "no")

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in an apprenticeship - she's far too young for that, she's still in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Let me continue with the assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about **your residency status** (as the claimant):

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

This is a key requirement for Child Benefit. You can also qualify if you meet certain special circumstances, such as:
- Having settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme (with certain conditions)
- Being a Crown servant living abroad
- Living in certain qualifying countries

Which of these applies to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm here in England.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Let me continue with the assessment:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to ask about your responsibility for Alex:

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

This means either:
- You live with Alex, OR
- You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the Child Benefit amount towards their care - for things like food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)

Do either of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to ask:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Another person who cares for Alex

Only one person can claim Child Benefit for each child at a time. If someone else is claiming, they would need to agree to transfer the claim to you.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Now I need to understand Alex's living situation:

**Which of the following describes Alex's current living arrangement?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (the usual situation)
2. **In hospital** (staying in hospital for medical treatment)
3. **In care** (being looked after by the local council)
4. **Fostered** (living with foster carers)

Which applies to Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex is living normally at home with me - just the usual situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
One final question about Alex:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

(This would typically be "no" if Alex is living at home with you and you're supporting her yourself)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything - I'm supporting her myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me now provide you with a complete assessment for both of your children:

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## **Your Child Benefit Eligibility Assessment**

### **Summary:**
✅ **You ARE eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex**  
❌ **You are NOT eligible to claim Child Benefit for Blake**

---

### **For Alex (8 years old): ELIGIBLE ✅**

Great news! Alex meets all the requirements for Child Benefit:
- She is under 16 years old
- She is not in paid work
- She doesn't receive benefits in her own name
- She's not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- You live in the UK (meeting residency requirements)
- You are responsible for her (she lives at home with you)
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for her
- The local council is not paying towards her care

### **For Blake (20 years old): NOT ELIGIBLE ❌**

Unfortunately, Blake does not qualify for Child Benefit because she has reached age 20. Child Benefit is only available for:
- Children under 16, OR
- Young people aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training

Once a child turns 20, they are no longer eligible regardless of their circumstances.

---

## **Next Steps for Alex:**

You can now apply for Child Benefit for Alex. Here's what to do:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate

**Important note about the High Income Child Benefit Charge:**
- If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the Child Benefit through the tax system
- You can still claim and receive the benefit, but may need to repay it via Self Assessment
- Alternatively, you can choose not to receive the payments but still register for Child Benefit to protect your National Insurance credits

**Payment information:**
- Payments are typically made every 4 weeks
- If you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits, you may be able to get weekly payments

**Apply here:** https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

---

Do you have any questions about your eligibility or the application process?

reasonings: Alex is ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. She meets all the qualifying criteria: (1) She is 8 years old, which is under the age limit of 16 for automatic qualification. (2) She is not in paid work and does not work 24+ hours per week. (3) She does not receive any out-of-work benefits in her own name. (4) She is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. (5) You (the claimant) normally live in the UK, meeting the residency requirement. (6) You are responsible for Alex as she lives at home with you. (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. (8) Alex is living normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostering). (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Blake is 20 years old, which exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility. Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or for those aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training. Once a child reaches age 20, they no longer qualify for Child Benefit, regardless of their circumstances.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has two children: Alex (8 years old) and Blake (20 years old). Alex meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit as she is under 16, lives at home with the claimant, and no disqualifying factors apply. Blake does not qualify due to exceeding the age limit of 19 (or just after 20th birthday in certain education circumstances). The claimant is eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex only.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

Blake is NOT ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit. Blake is 20 years old, which exceeds the maximum age limit for Child Benefit eligibility.
